ja-gram-n3-1675-fill-0: Likens one thing directly to another — he behaves the same way as many Americans, so the comparison calls for this pattern.
ja-gram-n3-516-fill-0: Rejects a blanket assumption — just because something is expensive doesn't automatically mean it's better. The sentence is making that point.
ja-gram-n3-516-fill-1: Pushes back on the idea that you can always get your own way — it's not guaranteed, and this pattern captures that soft denial.
ja-gram-n3-516-fill-2: Points out that making a good joke isn't reliably easy — it's not always the case, which is exactly what this pattern expresses.
ja-gram-n3-516-fill-3: The sentence challenges the idea that looking tasty equals tasting good — something isn't necessarily what it seems.
ja-gram-n3-516-fill-4: Money bringing happiness is treated as a general rule that doesn't always hold — this pattern flags that the assumption isn't absolute.
ja-gram-n3-1682-fill-0: Sets Wikipedia on equal footing with Britannica in terms of accuracy — matching two things to the same standard is what this pattern does.
ja-gram-n3-780-fill-0: A permission was granted given the specific situation (shooting from outside), so the speaker acted on that conditional — this pattern fits a situation-based 'if'.
ja-gram-n3-780-fill-1: The warning is tied directly to the situation of driving — if you're going to do that, then don't drink. A clear situational condition.
ja-gram-n3-780-fill-2: Frames concealing your beliefs as the condition being considered — given that situation, death would be preferable.
ja-gram-n3-780-fill-3: The refund policy kicks in only given a specific circumstance (unavoidable reasons) — the condition sets up the outcome.
ja-gram-n3-780-fill-4: Again a refund condition, but this time based on personal reasons — the policy depends on the situation presented.
ja-gram-n3-1368-fill-0: Watching the movie leads automatically and inevitably to tears — this pattern links a trigger to its natural, expected result.
ja-gram-n3-1368-fill-1: Leaving home immediately caused rain — the two events are sequenced as direct cause and automatic consequence.
ja-gram-n3-1368-fill-2: Being at this specific store is the trigger that makes the price possible — the consequence follows naturally from the situation.
ja-gram-n3-1368-fill-3: Winter arriving is a reliable, automatic trigger for rain — the sequence is a predictable natural consequence.
ja-gram-n3-1368-fill-4: Arriving at the station is the trigger that makes the phone call necessary — the obligation follows automatically from the event.
ja-gram-n3-1493-fill-0: The speaker is surprised and a little frustrated — the computer is new, yet already outdated. The noun-based form なのに fits after ばかり here.
ja-gram-n3-1493-fill-1: The information should be current but isn't — the sentence expresses surprise at that gap between expectation and reality.
ja-gram-n3-1493-fill-2: Brand new shoes needing repair already is unexpected — the contrast between 'new' and 'already broken' carries an emotional tone.
ja-gram-n3-1493-fill-3: Noticing you must have strong legs is the surprising conclusion from walking all that way — used here with だ before it to express the realization.
ja-gram-n3-1493-fill-4: Jim woke up and realized with some surprise that he was alone — the discovery has an unexpected quality to it.
ja-gram-n3-551-fill-0: Learning Japanese requires time — the sentence describes what time is needed for, not a contrast. This is the purpose usage.
ja-gram-n3-551-fill-1: The speaker is asking what time or place works for the purpose of meeting — a practical use of 'in order to'.
ja-gram-n3-551-fill-2: A passport is the required tool for the purpose of overseas travel — the sentence states what something is necessary for.
ja-gram-n3-551-fill-3: Swimwear has a purpose — it's what you use for swimming. The sentence states the function of something.
ja-gram-n3-551-fill-4: Five months is how long completing the job required — time or cost needed for a purpose is a classic use of this pattern.
ja-gram-n3-660-fill-0: The speaker eats fish but not meat — は sets up the implicit contrast between what is and isn't eaten.
ja-gram-n3-660-fill-1: She's alive, but the tone implies she's not well — は on 'alive' highlights it as the one true thing, contrasting with what's unspoken.
ja-gram-n3-660-fill-2: The speaker likes going to games, implying something else was just rejected — は marks 'going to baseball' as the one liked thing.
ja-gram-n3-660-fill-3: Liking addition but disliking subtraction — は appears on both for the contrast, and here marks the topic being contrasted.
ja-gram-n3-660-fill-4: He lived in Shanghai, which is the contrasting fact setting him apart from the war happening elsewhere.
ja-gram-n3-1481-fill-0: A simple standalone use of 反面 meaning 'on the other hand' — used here as a short reference example of the pattern.
ja-gram-n3-1481-fill-1: The brother is usually kind, but on the other side of his personality he can be mean — both traits coexist in the same person.
ja-gram-n3-1481-fill-2: The job pays well, but that positive is balanced by the demand of 12 working hours — two sides of the same reality.
ja-gram-n3-1481-fill-3: The cloth is strong against water but weak against heat — two opposite properties of the same material.
ja-gram-n3-1481-fill-4: The internet is useful for children's learning, but on the other side it can expose them to harmful content if unsupervised.
ja-gram-n3-1289-fill-0: He's old, yet he looks young — the result doesn't match what you'd expect given his age, which is exactly what this pattern highlights.
ja-gram-n3-1289-fill-1: Driving an old car is surprising for someone wealthy — the behavior doesn't match the category they belong to.
ja-gram-n3-1289-fill-2: Cold mornings are unexpected in June — you're measuring the weather against what June normally delivers.
ja-gram-n3-1289-fill-3: Vigour at 70 is beyond what you'd expect — the benchmark is his age, and the result exceeds it.
ja-gram-n3-1289-fill-4: The hotel is expensive compared to what you'd expect in this particular town — the town sets the standard, and the hotel exceeds it.
ja-gram-n3-410-fill-0: Even in the hypothetical scenario where marriage is possible, it still won't happen soon — assuming the best case, the conclusion still follows.
ja-gram-n3-410-fill-1: Even granting that the parents are famous, real talent is what actually earns recognition — the positive assumption doesn't change the conclusion.
ja-gram-n3-410-fill-2: Even assuming the other person is below you in rank, gratitude is still required — the assumption doesn't change the expected behavior.
ja-gram-n3-410-fill-3: Even being used as a gaming OS, Linux continues to evolve — the situation is assumed and the point made within it.
ja-gram-n3-410-fill-4: Even granting he's a nice person, the speaker still doesn't trust him — the concession is made, but the conclusion holds.
ja-gram-n3-1593-fill-0: The speaker wishes the good weather had carried over — trailing のに at sentence end expresses wistful regret about something that didn't happen.
ja-gram-n3-1593-fill-1: A longing for the rain to stop — のに at the end turns the hopeful condition into a soft, sighing wish.
ja-gram-n3-649-fill-0: The family has a fixed rule about which day to use, and にする expresses choosing and settling on that specific option.
ja-gram-n3-649-fill-1: Doing it 'this way' is the choice being made — にする marks the decision to go with a particular option or approach.
ja-gram-n3-1732-fill-0: The husband going to Tokyo wasn't his own free choice — it was arranged or decided by circumstances, which is what ことになる conveys.
ja-gram-n3-1732-fill-1: Studying abroad was arranged or settled — the speaker presents it as something that came to be decided, not as a personal vow.
ja-gram-n3-687-fill-1: Not taking care led to a state change — becoming sick is the natural result of a situation, expressed by になる.
ja-gram-n3-687-fill-2: A rough calculation reveals the situation will become terrible — になる describes the state things are heading toward.
ja-gram-n3-687-fill-3: Wanting to become a teacher is a future state the speaker aims for — になる expresses that desired transformation.
ja-gram-n3-1304-fill-0: Being too full to eat even a strawberry is an extreme case — さえ highlights the most minimal thing as an example of impossibility.
ja-gram-n3-1304-fill-1: Eating a mouse is the extreme thing humans might do when desperate — さえ marks it as the surprising outer limit of what's possible.
ja-gram-n3-1304-fill-2: Most people don't even know this thing exists — さえ marks existence itself as the minimum level of awareness that's being denied.
ja-gram-n3-1304-fill-3: They can't even find the source code — さえ emphasizes that finding it, the bare minimum step, is beyond them.
ja-gram-n3-1304-fill-4: Even with a ruler — the easiest aid available — straight lines are still impossible. さえ marks using a ruler as the minimal helpful thing.
ja-gram-n3-1199-fill-0: The speaker can only write romaji and nothing else — しか pairs with the negative verb to express that limitation.
ja-gram-n3-1199-fill-1: Only Mr. Tanaka came — everyone else is excluded. しか + negative verb expresses that no one outside this one person showed up.
ja-gram-n3-1199-fill-2: Having only 100 yen is a limiting constraint — しか with ない conveys the smallness of what's available.
ja-gram-n3-1199-fill-3: Nothing but red things exist here — everything else is absent. しか with ない expresses total exclusion of other options.
ja-gram-n3-1199-fill-4: The only way to get there is by car — no other method works. しか with the negative marks car as the sole route.
ja-gram-n3-253-fill-0: Love — not anything else — is specifically what strengthens people. こそ puts strong emphasis on love as the true source.
ja-gram-n3-253-fill-1: This time specifically is the moment the speaker is determined to succeed — こそ intensifies the resolve attached to 今度.
ja-gram-n3-253-fill-2: Dreams — and not other things — are singled out as the true source of hope. こそ gives dreams that emphatic, exclusive quality.
ja-gram-n3-253-fill-3: It is specifically the speaker, not the listener, who should apologize — こそ focuses attention sharply on わたし.
ja-gram-n3-253-fill-4: Family is singled out as truly the most important thing in life — こそ gives this the feeling of a deep, sincere declaration.
ja-gram-n3-1595-fill-0: It's warm to the degree that a heater is almost unnecessary — くらい measures how warm the evening is by expressing what that warmth equals.
ja-gram-n3-1595-fill-1: The tiredness was so extreme that walking even one step was impossible — くらい conveys the degree of exhaustion through its effect.
ja-gram-n3-1595-fill-2: Yamada is the benchmark for forgetfulness — no one forgets things to the degree Yamada does. くらい sets the scale.
ja-gram-n3-1595-fill-3: Cooking rice is the modest lower limit of what the speaker can do — くらい here marks the minimum level of ability.
ja-gram-n3-1595-fill-4: He is the only person capable enough for this job right now — くらい here marks his skill level as the threshold that fits.
ja-gram-n3-1502-fill-0: Just having arrived in Japan, the speaker was a newcomer — ばかり marks the period just after coming, when everything was unfamiliar.
ja-gram-n3-1502-fill-1: The speaker just ate a moment ago — ばかり expresses the very recent completion of eating, which is why waiting is needed.
ja-gram-n3-1502-fill-2: Just having started work, mistakes were natural — ばかり marks the period immediately after beginning, when everything is new.
ja-gram-n3-752-fill-0: The speaker is alerting the listener to something happening right now — よ signals new, urgent information being shared.
ja-gram-n3-752-fill-1: The speaker is asserting the seriousness of the situation directly to the listener — よ pushes the information as something they should register.
ja-gram-n3-752-fill-2: A personal confession delivered directly — よ here adds the assertive edge of saying 'I'm telling you this clearly'.
ja-gram-n3-752-fill-3: A direct command with assertive force — よ at the end makes it clear this is being said with intention, not gently.
ja-gram-n3-752-fill-4: A casual but firm agreement — よ gives the response a confident, assertive quality rather than hesitant.
ja-gram-n3-652-fill-0: Wishing out loud that tomorrow's fishing will go well — なあ adds the warm, daydreaming tone of hoping for something good.
ja-gram-n3-652-fill-1: A personal longing for a cold beer — なあ gives this the sighing, wistful feel of really wanting something in the moment.
ja-gram-n3-652-fill-2: Voicing hunger in a casual, expressive way — なあ softens it into a musing remark rather than a demand.
ja-gram-n3-879-fill-0: The proverb is being quoted or referenced — って marks it as something people say, a common expression being cited.
ja-gram-n3-879-fill-1: The speaker is reporting what they actually said in the past — って marks a direct or close quote of spoken words.
ja-gram-n3-879-fill-2: Casually confirming whether this is the key he was looking for — って rephrases what was said or understood, asking for confirmation.
ja-gram-n3-879-fill-3: Reporting that someone said they didn't know — って here relays a statement, functioning like 'said that' or 'told me'.
ja-gram-n3-879-fill-4: No matter how much someone begs, the answer is no — って here means 'no matter what (is asked/said)'.
ja-gram-n3-776-fill-0: A mild, casual female affirmation — わ softens the agreement while still expressing genuine feeling.
ja-gram-n3-776-fill-1: Sharing an impression about the book with the listener — わ adds a soft, gentle emphasis typical of female casual speech.
ja-gram-n3-776-fill-2: The speaker is expressing mild distress and resignation at the cash register — わ gives the exclamations that soft, personal female tone.
ja-gram-n3-1516-fill-0: The speaker is repeating their stated intention with emotion after being doubted — ってば insists on what was already said.
ja-gram-n3-1516-fill-1: The instruction to go home is being repeated with clear frustration — ってば shows the speaker is fed up with not being heard.
ja-gram-n3-1516-fill-2: An emphatic, emotionally charged exclamation — ってば here adds a sharp insistence to the question.
ja-gram-n3-1516-fill-3: The speaker is insisting that Marle is fine, probably after being challenged — ってば conveys 'I already told you this!'.
ja-gram-n3-1686-fill-0: Returning home after a long absence — ぶり marks the time span that passed since the last visit.
ja-gram-n3-1686-fill-1: Coming back to Japan after a three-year gap — ぶり attaches to the time span to say this is the first time in that period.
ja-gram-n3-868-fill-0: Mid-conversation the speaker shifts topic to suggest food — ところで signals that clean break to a new subject.
ja-gram-n3-868-fill-1: Even trying to persuade him wouldn't work — this alternate usage means 'no matter what' rather than a topic shift.
ja-gram-n3-868-fill-2: Moving from the current conversation to a new question about the person's job — ところで marks the casual topic change.
ja-gram-n3-868-fill-3: Pivoting from whatever was being discussed to ask about homework — ところで introduces the new topic politely.
ja-gram-n3-868-fill-4: The speaker has something to say and uses ところで to open the new subject naturally.
ja-gram-n3-1711-fill-3: Rain started, and on top of that strong wind arrived — その上 adds the second bad thing piling on top of the first.
ja-gram-n3-1711-fill-4: He speaks English, and additionally French — その上 adds the second language as an extra on top of what was already stated.
ja-gram-n3-1706-fill-0: The consequence of not apologizing is anger — さもないと warns that the bad outcome will happen if the action isn't taken.
ja-gram-n3-1706-fill-1: Missing the bus is the consequence of not getting on — さもないと connects the required action to its negative alternative.
ja-gram-n3-1706-fill-2: Missing the bus follows from not hurrying — さもないと makes the warning explicit: do it, or face the consequence.
ja-gram-n3-1706-fill-3: Not buttoning up will lead to catching cold — さもないと states the warning plainly after the instruction.
ja-gram-n3-1706-fill-4: Failing the test is what happens if you don't study — さもないと links the command to the consequence of ignoring it.
ja-gram-n3-1520-fill-0: Admitting something to yourself is hard; admitting it to others is even harder — ましてや escalates from the first to the more extreme case.
ja-gram-n3-1520-fill-1: Can't play guitar, and violin is an even bigger stretch — ましてや escalates to the more difficult instrument.
ja-gram-n3-1520-fill-2: Won't even lend the watch, let alone give it away — ましてや takes an already firm position and escalates it further.
ja-gram-n3-1520-fill-3: Can't afford a used car, and a new one is completely out of the question — ましてや escalates to the more extreme option.
ja-gram-n3-1520-fill-4: Already speaks French, and English is even more within reach — ましてや here escalates positively to the even stronger case.
ja-gram-n3-1407-fill-0: Learning there's a 9 o'clock meeting, the speaker draws an immediate conclusion: no time for beer — そうすると reasons from new information.
ja-gram-n3-1407-fill-1: Given the situation just described, that outcome follows — そうすると draws a conclusion based on the preceding context.
ja-gram-n3-1681-fill-0: What's good for the mother's body flows through and benefits the baby too — ひいては shows how one effect extends to a downstream result.
ja-gram-n3-1681-fill-1: The first effect might lead further to internationalization — ひいては connects the immediate outcome to its broader, downstream consequence.
